content.text:
Geographic Information Systems
Semester:
3  |
Type:
UEF32  |
VHS:
63h  |
Weekly hours:
C: 1h30 / TD: 1h30 / TP: 1h30  |
Personal work VHS:
42h  |
Coefficient:
3  |
Credits:
4
Teaching Objectives
Introduce students to the fundamental concepts and tools of geomatics, enabling them to collect, organise, store, analyse and visualise geographic data (satellite images or field-collected data). The programme includes 2D and 3D modelling of geo-spatial data for decision support, GIS tools, and an introduction to spatial analysis and cartography.
Recommended prior knowledge:
Databases, probability and statistics, graph theory.
Course Content (Lectures: 21h)
Introduction to GIS
Definition of a GIS: key concepts, history, and its role in geomatics
Uses and challenges of GIS (urban planning, environment, finance, decision support, human resources, etc.)
GIS components: hardware, software, data, methods, and human resources
Geographic Data Modelling: Data in a GIS
Data types: raster and vector data, their structures, and representations
Data sources: acquisition, management and storage of geographic data
Fundamental spatial notions: geographic reference systems, geographic coordinates
Data quality: precision, consistency, and integrity
Cartography and Data Representation
Basic cartography principles: symbolisation, legend, scale
Creation of thematic and custom maps
Data visualisation: 2D and 3D representation, animation and interaction
Spatial Analysis and Decision-Making
Proximity, neighbourhood, distribution analysis, etc.
Practical GIS applications in various fields (urban management, environmental management, transport, etc.)
GIS Design
Presentation of common GIS software (ArcGIS, QGIS, etc.)
Basic functionalities: creation, visualisation, management, and analysis of spatial data
Data manipulation and querying: spatial queries, data joins
Spatial analysis and map creation: overlay, zoning, proximity, and other analysis techniques
The GPS System
Presentation; types of measurements; application examples
Assessment
In-person semester exam (60%)
Continuous assessment (CC) (40%): continuous tests, personal work
Practical work:
Practice of acquired knowledge through concrete exercises; real case studies; use of GIS software for spatial analyses and map creation.
References
Jean-Philippe Giraud (2010).
Principes de Géomatique
. Lavoisier.
Jean-Philippe Giraud (2004).
Systèmes d'Information Géographique
. Hermès Science.
Denègre, Jean et Salgé, François (2001).
Les systèmes d'information géographiques
. PUF, 2ème édition.
Laurini, R., and D. Thompson (1992).
Fundamentals of spatial information systems
. Academic Press.
Burroughs, P. A. (1986).
Geographical information systems for land resources assessment
. Clarendon Press.
